coredata.dc:title: Chicken egg white as a “greener” biomass source for the rapid synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Cardoso-Ávila, P.E.
Pichardo-Molina, J.L.
Vázquez-Olmos, M.
González-Aguiñaga, E.
coredata.dc:description: Most of the methodologies reported to fabricate fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are not fully sustainable. Here, we report the “green” optimization of our base catalysis method; by using chicken egg white powder, we obtained CDs with a 25.6% fluorescence quantum yield in a one-step synthesis that completes in 6 h and do not require any purification. This improved methodology is faster, cheaper and has a better mass and solvents economy, so it is way “greener”. The effect of three hydroxides was studied and z-potential, UV–Vis, fluorescence and FTIR spectroscopies, and TEM were used as characterization techniques.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Albumin
Chicken egg white
Fluorescent carbon dots
Green chemistry
Organic nanoparticles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis absorption spectra KOH, LiOH and NaOH sets is shown in panels A, B and C, respectively. The CDs colorations are show in panel D, blue labels indicate the pH after the synthesis. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fluorescence maps for the samples with the higher QY for each hydroxide (A, B and C). The QY as function of the hydroxide concentration (D).
FTIR spectra of selected CDs samples (A, B and C), the Z-potentials for all the CDs (D), TEM image of KOH3 (E), and a resume of the albumin-based CDs reported previously in the literature (F).

head.ce:title: Chicken egg white as a “greener” biomass source for the rapid synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Most of the methodologies reported to fabricate fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are not fully sustainable. Here, we report the “green” optimization of our base catalysis method; by using chicken egg white powder, we obtained CDs with a 25.6% fluorescence quantum yield in a one-step synthesis that completes in 6 h and do not require any purification. This improved methodology is faster, cheaper and has a better mass and solvents economy, so it is way “greener”. The effect of three hydroxides was studied and z-potential, UV–Vis, fluorescence and FTIR spectroscopies, and TEM were used as characterization techniques.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles or carbon dots (CDs) have emerged in recent years as promising nanomaterials for chemo- and bio-sensing, imaging, drug delivery and in photovoltaic devices. Many reports use a large variety of carbon sources to produce CDs by arc-discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical synthesis, organic pyrolysis, microwave assisted or hydro- and solvothermal methods [1], but they usually require a lot of thermal or electrical energy since the reaction temperatures surpass 200 °C. For many reasons, the sustainability of these methods is not accomplished, thus, the green chemistry requirements are not fully meet [2]. However, “greener” methods can be achieved by means of selecting better biomass sources, designing synthetic routes that do not require external energy supply, and avoiding the need of nanomaterials purification after their synthesis; In short, it is necessary to consider the carbon footprint (CFP) of each step during the fabrication of the nanomaterial, in its use and at subsequent disposal.

Among the biomass sources used we can mention organisms, waste materials and protein products, each type with pros and cons. In the wide variety of proteins available, albumins stand out for being widely studied [3]. Bovine serum albumin (BSA) is an easily available protein, since it is used in many bio-protocols, and it was previously reported as biomass source for the synthesis of CDs by solvo- or hydro-thermal [4–6], acid hydrolysis [7] and base catalysis [8] methodologies. However, BSA is obtained from the cattle blood, so, it must be purified by physical and chemical processes to meet grades required for the different applications; for this reason, its CFP is something to take into account.

On the other hand, chicken eggs are easily available, one of the cheapest protein sources and have a reduced CFP compared to cattle. Chicken egg white (CEW) is 84–89 % water [9], the rest of the solids are mainly ovoalbumin (54 %), ovotransferrin (12 %), ovomucoid (11 %) and Lysozyme (3.4 %) [10]; so it is clear that using CEW directly from the eggs increases the variability of the products for many fabrication methodologies, however, it has been used in the rapid CDs fabrication by plasma-induced pyrolysis [11], in the synthesis of highly fluorescent gold nanoclusters [12], and in the direct hydrothermal synthesis of CEW into CDs [13]. CEW was also freeze-dried and used to obtain CDs under alkali conditions in an 80 h incubation process, followed by pH neutralization and dialysis purification [14].

Even though BSA and CEW could be considered “green” biomass sources and have been used to fabricate fluorescent CDs, most of the methodologies mentioned before are not fully sustainable. Here, we report the optimization of our previously reported direct one-pot room-temperature base catalysis synthesis [8]; by using food-grade CEW powder, we were able to obtain CDs with a maximum 25.6 % fluorescence quantum yield (QY) in a synthesis that completes in 6 h and do not require any purification. This improved methodology is faster, cheaper and has a better mass and solvents economy, so it could be considered way “greener”. The effect of three hydroxides during the synthesis was studied and z-potential, UV–Vis, fluorescence and FTIR spectroscopies, and TEM were used as characterization techniques.

Our previously reported method [8], was optimized following the green chemistry precepts. Briefly, 8 mL of food-grade CEW powder stock solution (0.5 % M/V, MCS, Mexico) were mixed with 1 mL of hydroxide solution (KOH, LiOH or NaOH, >95 %, Sigma) at concentrations varying from 0.1 to 0.6 M under magnetic stirring, followed by the dropwise injection of 16 mL of ethanol at 10 mL/min rate (99.5 %). Finally, 160 µL of glutaraldehyde (25 % solution, Sigma) was added and allowed to react for 5 min, the solution was placed in a rotary shaker for 6 h, after which the synthesis was completed and no further purification was required. Samples were labeled as KOHx, LiOHx and NaOHx, with x corresponding to the hydroxide’s concentrations.

UV–vis spectra were acquired by a fiber-optic spectrometer (EPP2000, Stellarnet). Fluorescence maps were measured in an Edinburg Instruments FS5 fluorometer with 2 and 4 nm bandwidths for excitation and emission, respectively. Excitation varied from 300 to 550 nm, and emission started with a 10 nm offset from the excitation. The absolute quantum yield (QY) was measured by the integrating sphere module SC30 and its method: the spectra was captured in the 520–750 nm range with 530 ± 6 nm excitation and 0.25 nm emission bandwidth. FTIR spectra were acquired using a Cary660 spectrometer (Agilent Technologies). Z-potentials were measured in a Zetasizer Nano-ZS (Malvern-Panalytical). TEM was carried out in a JEM-2100 JEOL microscope operated at 200 kV.

A control sample was prepared without any hydroxide; it did not show fluorescence and coagulated shortly after prepared. All CDs samples showed colorations going from yellow to pink hues, as depicted in Fig. 1 D; and most of the samples resulted with neutral pHs. For KOH and LiOH sets, only samples with 0.6 M had a pH 8 and 11, respectively; while for NaOH, samples from 0.4 to 0.6 M had pH 11. Fig. 1A-C show the absorption spectra for the three CDs sample sets; it can be seen that for all CDs there are two absorption bands, one around 300 nm that widened with the increment of the hydroxides. The subpanel in Fig. 1A shows that the 530 nm absorption band maximized for KOH5, while in the LiOH set the absorption was maximized for 0.3 M and then decreased disorderly for the LiOH4-6 samples (subpanel Fig. 1B). In the same way, the absorption was maximized for NaOH3 and then decreased.

The fluorescence analysis was made in such a way that both absorption peaks were excited. The excitation-emission maps of some selected samples are shown in Fig. 2 . KOH3 presented an excitation-dependent behavior: the principal emission band was centered at 555 nm when excited from 450 to 540 nm. There were also two other less intense emissions at 450 and 500 nm at lower excitations. LiOH2 and NaOH2 maps showed a similar trend, maximum emission centered at 555 nm and two other secondary less intense emissions. With these results we can assure that the fluorescence features of these CDs are not related with the hydroxide used, but they are completely associated with the methodology used for its synthesis, since our previous works using BSA and NaOH also resulted in 555 nm emission CDs [8]. The QYs are presented in Fig. 2D; for the KOH there was an increase from 20.1 % in KOH1 to 25.6 % for KOH3 and then a slow decrease to 17.7 % for the final concentration tested. While LiOH and NaOH the maximum QY was reached at 0.2 M, 23.3 and 24.2 %, respectively; but a fast decay was observed for both hydroxides going as low as 10 and 8.3 % at 0.6 M. This indicates that while the three hydroxides tested had no effect in the emission wavelength, their relative alkaline strengths did influence the optimum concentration range for maximizing the QY. It is remarkable that with the use of low purity and food-grade reactants we were able to obtain CDs with good fluorescence performance, for instance, QY only diminished 1.5 % with respect the BSA-based CDs that we previously reported, but also, we shortened the times of synthesis and reduced the volume of the solvent used.

FTIR analysis revealed vibrations at 3294, 2937, 1650, 1560 and 1396 cm−1, that are associated with the stretching modes of –OH, –CH2, –CO (amide I), –CN (amide II) and –CN (amide III) [15,16], for all samples regardless the hydroxide used (Fig. 3 ). The Z-potentials exceeded −30 mV in all cases ensuring the colloidal stability of the CDs synthesized, but specially the KOH set had all close to −40 mV. TEM revealed monodisperse CDs with an 8.4 ± 1.6 nm mean size for KOH3 sample (Fig. 3E), which is in agreement with the sizes reported previously for albumin-based CDs.

Several reports in the existing literature had used Albumins, BSA or CEW, in hydrothermal methodologies to produce QDs with QYs as high as 64 and 34.8 % (see table in Fig. 3F), but these methodologies are slow and with high energy and reactants usage, not to mention the purification processes. On the other hand, base catalysis methodologies can be optimized to be fast, performed at room temperature and with low reactant consumption, reaching QYs in the 16.8 to 27.1 % range.

In this work, we showed that by using food-grade CEW powder as biomass source, it is possible to fabricate fluorescent CDs with good physical (neutral pHs, good colloidal stability) and optical properties (two absorption bands, yellow emission, +20 % QYs) in a simple, cheap and fast methodology that do not an external energy supply, special equipment or further purification of the CDs. The fluorescent properties of the CDs were not related with the type of the hydroxide used, since all samples had the distinctive 530 nm absorption and maximum emission at 555 nm; and the best synthesis conditions were on 0.1–0.3 M range for the three hydroxides.

P.E. Cardoso-Ávila: . J.L. Pichardo-Molina: . M. Vázquez-Olmos: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – original draft. E. González-Aguiñaga: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thanks to M.C. Albor for her technical assistance, the Materials Characterization Laboratory (CIO) and Dr. Ojeda-Galvan (UASLP) for the TEM analysis.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: M.L. Liu, et al., Green Chem. 21 (2019) 449, https://doi.org/10.1039/c8gc02736f.
T.P.H. Hutapea, et al., JS: AMD, 8 (2) 2023, 100549, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jsamd.2023.100549.
